first-pagaraphs: The map shows where the person on probation has been, using signals (‘pings’) sent from their GPS tag.
The tag uses satellite signals to record its location. It usually pings around once a minute, sending this location information to the Electronic Monitoring Service.
Each ping appears as a point on the map that indicates the person’s most likely location.
You can filter the map to view the person’s movements for specific dates and times. The most recent activity shown is about 10 minutes behind real time.

locationAccuracy.title: Location accuracy

image.alt: Map with a blue circle with a dot in its centre attached to a bent line with arrows on it

first-pagaraphs: Location accuracy can vary. It may be reduced if, for example, satellite signals are blocked or the tag’s battery is low.
Each map point is surrounded by a ‘confidence circle’ that shows how certain the system is about the person’s location.
The confidence circle covers the area where the tag may have been when it pinged, with the most likely location at the centre.
The smaller the circle, the more certainty there is that the location shown is accurate.
locationAccuracy.insert: The confidence circles are around 95% accurate. This means that the true location of each ping will fall within the confidence circle around 95% of the time.
last-pagaraphs: To view the exact size of a confidence circle, select the point at its centre to open the ping card (pop-up box).
Under ‘Accuracy’, you can view the distance from the centre to the edge of the circle, measured in metres.
You can add or remove confidence circles from the map using the ‘View location accuracy’ checkbox in the map controls.

directionOfTravel.title: Direction of travel

image.alt: Map with a blue line with black arrows and 2 blue dots on it that runs alongside a street

first-pagaraphs: Each point is joined to the next point in the sequence to form a trail.
directionOfTravel.insert: The trail is formed by joining the points with a straight line. It's aimed at making the points easier to view and is not intended to show the actual route taken.
last-pagaraphs: Arrows along the trail show the estimated direction of travel, based on the order of the location points.
You can view the order of the points by selecting ‘Point numbers’ in the map controls.
To add or remove the arrows, use the 'Direction of travel' checkbox in the map controls.

dateAndTime.title: Date and time of activity

image.alt: Box containing a number and values for 'Accuracy', 'Date, time', 'Lat, long', 'Speed', 'Signal type'

first-pagaraphs: Each point has a timestamp that shows when the tag pinged in that location.
To view the timestamp, select the point and open its ping card.

speedTravel.title: Speed of travel

image.alt: Box containing a number and values for 'Accuracy', 'Date, time', 'Lat, long', 'Speed', 'Signal type’

first-pagaraphs: To view the estimated speed of travel, select a point to open its ping card.
The value shown reflects the estimated speed of travel at the moment the tag pinged. It does not indicate the speed of travel between points.

signalType.title: Signal type (GPS or LBS)

image.alt: Box containing a number and values for 'Accuracy', 'Date, time', 'Lat, long', 'Speed', 'Signal type'

first-pagaraphs: When the tag is unable to connect to GPS satellite signals, it uses local cellular network towers to estimate its location instead.
This is known as LBS (location-based services) tracking.
Because cellular towers are spaced further apart than GPS satellites, LBS is less accurate than GPS tracking.
With LBS tracking, the tag will usually ping around once every 3 minutes.
To check which signal type was used to detect a location, select the point to open its ping card.

missingTrailData.title: Missing trail data

image.alt: Map with a blue dotted line with arrows on it, which starts on a grey line then runs across a green area

first-pagaraphs: Missing trail data usually means that either the tag is not working or that it cannot get a signal.
Missing data is shown on the map by a dotted line.
A gap of more than 5 minutes between two points in a sequence also indicates that data is missing.
